content.full_text:
/

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 3, 1988

Peter Bagatelos
Bagatelos & Padem
601 California St., Ste. 1801
San Francisco, CA 94108

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-88-395

Dear Mr. Bagatelos:

You have requested informal assistance with regard to the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

Your summary of my October 11, 1988, telephone assistance is
correct, as follows:

1. A recipient committee which provides full and
adequate consideration for payments received from participants
in a joint mailing has not received "contributions" from the
participants and, therefore, will disclose the payments on
Schedule G of the Form 420 or 490 as "miscellaneous increases
to cash." (Section 82015.)

2. Regulation 18435 states that the sender of a mass
mailing is the "candidate or committee who pays for the
largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing,
printing and posting of the mailing."

3. Committees primarily formed to support or oppose a
ballot measure are specifically excluded from the definition
of "slate mailer organization" (Section 82048.5(b)(4)) and are
not required to comply with the identification or disclaimer
requirements of Section 84305.5 for mailings sent by such
organizations.

_________________________________

1/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Peter Bagatelos
Page Two

     I also concur with your conclusion that a ballot measure
committee is making contributions when it includes non-paying
candidates or measures in a mailing at the behest of the
non-paying candidates or ballot measure committees. (Section
82015.)

     If you have additional questions, please call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Carla Wardlow
                                            Political Reform Consultant

LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 1801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM

PETER A BAGATELOS

WES VAN WINKLE

TELEPHONE
(415) 982-7100
FAX
(415) 982-1085

October 12, 1988

Ms. Carla Wardlow
Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Carla:

This will confirm our telephone conversation on October 11,
1988. I asked you whether there are any particular disclaimer
or identification requirements for a committee primarily formed
to support or oppose a ballot measure, which also sends a slate
mailer. The slate mailer would include a number of candidates
and/or measures, some of whom will pay for a portion of the
mailer.

I advised you that my interpretation is that this is in the
nature of a joint mailing on the part of two or more
committees. As such, the ballot measure committee would report
the receipt of monies from some or all of the participants in
the mailer as miscellaneous receipts on Schedule C of its Form
420. Pursuant to the definition of who is a "sender" under
Regulation Section 18435, the committee which pays the greatest
amount of the cost of the mailing would be identified as the
sender in at least 6-point type on the outside envelope and on
at least one insert. The provisions applying to slate mailer
organizations, as defined in the Political Reform Act, would not
apply to such ballot measure committee, because of the
exclusion contained in Government Code Section 82048.5(b)(4).
Therefore, there would be no asterisk or other disclaimer
requirements applicable to such a mass mailing.

You indicated that you concurred with the above analysis.

We did not discuss in this telephone conversation, but we
have discussed previously, that where there is coordination
between the ballot measure committee and the other participants
who do not pay for a place on the mass mailing, then there would
be a non-monetary contribution by the ballot measure committee
to those other campaign committees.

Thank you very much for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

Peter A. Bagatelos

PAB/yjw

LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM
PETER A. BAGATELOS

October 12, 1988

Ms. Carla Wardlow
Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Carla:

     This will confirm our telephone conversation on October 11,
1988. I asked you whether there are any particular disclaimer
or identification requirements for a committee primarily formed
to support or oppose a ballot measure, a which also sends a slate
mailer. The slate mailer would include a number of candidates
and/or measures, some of whom will pay for a portion of the
mailer.

     I advised you that my interpretation is that this is in the
nature of a joint mailing on the part of two or more
committees. As such, the ballot measure committee would report
the receipt of monies from some or all of the participants in
the mailer as miscellaneous receipts on Schedule G of its Form
420. Pursuant to the definition of who is a "sender" under
Regulation Section 18435, the committee which pays the greatest
amount of the cost of the mailing would be identified as the
sender in at least 6-point type on the outside envelope and on
at least one insert. The provisions applying to slate mailer
organizations, as defined in the Political Reform Act, would not
apply to such a ballot measure committee, because of the
exclusion contained in Government Code Section 82048.5(b)(4).
Therefore, there would be no asterisk or other disclaimer
requirements applicable to such a mass mailing.

     You indicated that you concurred with the above analysis.

     We did not discuss in this telephone conversation, but we
have discussed previously, that where there is coordination
between the ballot measure committee and the other participants
who do not pay for a place on the mass mailing, then there would
be a non-monetary contribution by the ballot measure committee
to those other campaign committees.

     Thank you very much for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

Peter A. Bagatelos

PAB/yjw

sections.question: Whether there are any particular disclaimer or identification requirements for a committee primarily formed to support or oppose a ballot measure, which also sends a slate mailer that includes a number of candidates and/or measures, some of whom will pay for a portion of the mailer.
sections.conclusion: 1. A recipient committee which provides full and adequate consideration for payments received from participants in a joint mailing has not received 'contributions' from the participants and, therefore, will disclose the payments on Schedule G of the Form 420 or 490 as 'miscellaneous increases to cash.' (Section 82015.) 2. Regulation 18435 states that the sender of a mass mailing is the 'candidate or committee who pays for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing, printing and posting of the mailing.' 3. Committees primarily formed to support or oppose a ballot measure are specifically excluded from the definition of 'slate mailer organization' (Section 82048.5(b)(4)) and are not required to comply with the identification or disclaimer requirements of section 84305.5 for mailings sent by such organizations. 4. A ballot measure committee is making contributions when it includes non-paying candidates or measures in a mailing at the behest of the non-paying candidates or ballot measure committees.
sections.question_synthetic: What disclosure and identification requirements apply when a ballot measure committee sends a slate mailer with multiple candidates and measures, some of which contribute to the cost?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ballot measure committees sending joint slate mailers should report participant payments as miscellaneous receipts on Schedule G, identify the largest cost-bearing participant as the sender, and are exempt from slate mailer organization disclaimer requirements; however, they make contributions when including non-paying participants at those participants' request.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter from Bagatelos (Oct 12) and FPPC response letter from Carla Wardlow (Nov 3). The response letter confirms telephone advice and provides four numbered conclusions. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers but content clearly corresponds to these categories.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether there are any particular disclaimer or identification requirements for a committee primarily formed to support or oppose a ballot measure, which also sends a slate mailer that includes a number of candidates and/or measures, some of whom will pay for a portion of the mailer.

CONCLUSION: 1. A recipient committee which provides full and adequate consideration for payments received from participants in a joint mailing has not received 'contributions' from the participants and, therefore, will disclose the payments on Schedule G of the Form 420 or 490 as 'miscellaneous increases to cash.' (Section 82015.) 2. Regulation 18435 states that the sender of a mass mailing is the 'candidate or committee who pays for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing, printing and posting of the mailing.' 3. Committees primarily formed to support or oppose a ballot measure are specifically excluded from the definition of 'slate mailer organization' (Section 82048.5(b)(4)) and are not required to comply with the identification or disclaimer requirements of section 84305.5 for mailings sent by such organizations. 4. A ballot measure committee is making contributions when it includes non-paying candidates or measures in a mailing at the behest of the non-paying candidates or ballot measure committees.

embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter confirming that ballot measure committees sending slate mailers with multiple candidates and measures must report participant payments as miscellaneous receipts, identify the largest cost-bearing participant as sender, and are exempt from slate mailer organization disclaimer requirements, though coordination with non-paying participants may constitute contributions.
